ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'chicken soup' class (FOODON_03302050); no specific entry for herbal black chicken (Silkie) soup exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ covers chicken soup generically; no dedicated code for TCM herbal Silkie chicken soup. Closest parent code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for herbal black chicken soup as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of herbal black chicken soup is approximately one bowl of 350 g, comprising broth, chicken pieces, and softened herbs.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken soup, homemade / traditional preparation — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for chicken broth and cooked chicken (NDB 06172, 05064)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database provides the closest regional match for Chinese-style chicken soup. Core macros (energy 85 kcal, protein 9.5 g, fat 4.8 g, carbohydrate 1.2 g per 100 g) are consistent with HPB chicken soup entries and USDA SR Legacy data for homemade chicken broth with chicken meat. Micronutrient values cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chicken broth + cooked Silkie-equivalent chicken). Saturated fat estimated at 1.4 g per 100 g based on USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken and broth fat profile. Values are plausible for a slow-simmered herbal chicken soup.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on chicken soup / broth with low carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for herbal black chicken soup. GI estimated at 25 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for chicken soup / broth, which is a high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate food. Glycemic load per 350 g serving calculated as (25 × 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 3.5) / 100 = 1.05, rounded to 0.1 per 100 g basis; per serving GL ≈ 1.1 (low). The very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g) means GI has minimal practical relevance.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (120 mg per 100 g); high potassium (180 mg per 100 g) supports favourable sodium-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Herbal black chicken soup contains a moderate 120 mg sodium per 100 g (420 mg per 350 g serving); individuals with hypertension should prepare the soup without added salt and limit portion size to one bowl to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g) and high protein (9.5 g per 100 g) support postprandial glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a low estimated glycaemic index of 25, herbal black chicken soup has negligible impact on blood glucose and is a suitable protein-rich meal option for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate total fat (4.8 g per 100 g) with estimated saturated fat of 1.4 g per 100 g; lean protein source with potassium and selenium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Herbal black chicken soup provides lean protein with relatively low saturated fat and is rich in potassium and selenium, making it a heart-friendly soup option; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid adding extra salt or oil during preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Antioxidant compounds from TCM herbs (goji berries, astragalus root) and anti-inflammatory potential of Silkie chicken collagen
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The TCM herbs used in this soup, including goji berries and astragalus root, provide antioxidants that may support management of inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis; the soft texture also makes it suitable for individuals with reduced jaw mobility.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herbal Black Chicken Soup (乌鸡汤 / 烏雞湯, Wūjītāng) is a traditional Chinese medicinal soup made by slow-simmering or double-steaming Silkie chicken — a breed prized for its black skin, bones, and dark meat — with a blend of Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) herbs including goji berries, red dates (jujube), astragalus root (huang qi), dried Chinese yam (shan yao), and angelica sinensis (dang gui). The resulting broth is deeply aromatic, mildly sweet, and dark in colour, with a rich umami flavour and tender chicken pieces. The soup is a staple of Cantonese and broader Chinese culinary-medicinal tradition, commonly consumed as a restorative meal during convalescence, postpartum recovery, or periods of fatigue. Nutritionally, it provides approximately 85 kcal per 100 g, with 9.5 g protein, 4.8 g fat, and only 1.2 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate soup. It is a good source of niacin (4.5 mg), vitamin B12 (1.5 mcg), selenium (15.5 mcg), potassium (180 mg), and phosphorus (120 mg). The very low carbohydrate content and high protein make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension (when prepared without added salt), and cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal-compatible. A standard serving is approximately one bowl of 350 g.
common_user_queries: What is herbal black chicken soup?
Is Wu Ji Tang good for diabetics?
What herbs are in black chicken soup?
Can I eat herbal chicken soup if I have high blood pressure?
What is 乌鸡汤 in English?
Is black chicken soup good for recovery after illness?

confidence_reason: Core macros are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo chicken soup entries and USDA SR Legacy data for homemade chicken broth with chicken meat. No exact HPB entry for herbal black chicken (Silkie) soup was found; values are adapted from the closest regional and USDA equivalents. Micronutrient values are plausible but estimated. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database chicken soup category. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo (Chinese-style chicken soup) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (chicken broth NDB 06172; cooked chicken NDB 05064) as no exact entry for herbal Silkie black chicken soup exists in any approved primary source. Saturated fat (1.4 g), monounsaturated fat (1.9 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g), cholesterol (45 mg), and water (83.0 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy chicken soup / broth fat profile and are marked as adapted. 2. Vitamin A is reported as mcg RAE (13.5 mcg RAE); the input value of 45.0 mcg likely refers to mcg retinol activity equivalents from mixed animal and herb sources — converted and retained at 13.5 mcg RAE consistent with USDA SR Legacy cooked chicken values. 3. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as chicken-based soups contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy. 4. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database chicken soup / broth category; no direct GI measurement for herbal black chicken soup is available. 5. The input contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving (350g)' entries); deduplicated to a single 350 g serving. 6. The input source 'Chinese Medicine Journal / cmjournal.org' is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence; all nutrient values are grounded in HPB Singapore and USDA approved sources. 7. FoodOn match FOODON_03302050 ('chicken soup') and FoodEx2 code A00GJ ('Chicken soup') are closest parent matches; no exact ontology code exists for herbal Silkie black chicken soup.
